feedback.text: Though I'm not a full EU-Citizen as a Norwegian, I'm still heavily affected by the EU and its benefits and flaws. This is a hopefull request to shift your focus towards Video games, and hopefully prevent their cultural erasure: I have for a long time seen digital media that people have purchased become non functional. Digital products that did not have a specific end of life date, but which companies shut down from the server side. Personally I've only been burned once by this when I bought the game City of Heroes. After having bought it from the store, I waited to install it until a week had passed by. In that time they had decided to shut down the game servers, making my game and CD useless. We want games to not vanish. We are loosing functionalities of company made products we have purchased. Games we expected would work for many years. Unlike physical media, the digital is reliant on the companies servers. Though it's unreasonable to expect server functionality for games forever, it is currently a free for all for companies to publish and shut down games as they please. Though they won't currently do this often since their reputation could take a heavy hit. But right now there are several instances of games that could end up loosing functionality on the companies whim. Not only because the companies can shut down the servers, but also because they sometimes don't preserve any info or code from the servers, making it extremely hard to potentially relaunch the game in a functional state. They do this of course since there is no profit in the game anymore, but permanently erasing the very potential of running the old games is erasure of a small part of our culture. Is it an important part of culture? Well for me it is. I want people to be able to look back and see how we have developed and changed. We see it in movies, books, art and museum artifacts. Weather depicting fiction or historic accuracy, it shows our past, what we valued, how we lived and what we wanted to share with others. More and more media stops being on physical CDs, and the trends slowly shifts towards most things becoming exclusively digital. Books, movies, games. It slowly becomes more common that the consumer is not in control of the media they consumes. Weather it is preserved, available or deleted/removed. One particularly big and popular game company has updated their End-User License Agreement, in witch a part of it says: "Those who own the product must destroy all copies at all cost" when the EULA terminates. Though there is no current way for them to enforce the deletion of the remaining files on someone's gaming machine, it could potentially be something they can enforce in the future, depending on how laws develop. Less old functional games would mean more purchases of new ones after all. Weather the games depict history, demand I reflect on different values, or are simply entertaining and pretty. I still want it available in some form to those who come after me. Weather they hate or love it, I want people to have the opportunity to experience it, to know what their current culture is built from. Just as I read history and watch movies mad in my parents past, understanding what today's world came from. Some people have taken upon themselves to restore functionality to old games. I have seen videos of people playing the old, abandoned City of Heroes, the game I once bought. They figured out how to make a personal computer be the server, but it is currently illegal to do so. Though I definitely can't blame them as I myself experienced buying the product with a few more days of functionality, that realistically should have been years. I might not be completely happy with whatever solution that eventually will protect and preserve old media, but it's better to put trust in groups and people than fighting for it alone. I hope and wish you will consider game preservation and function as part of the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you
